bank: Reviews with higher lexical diversity are more likely truthful.
Reviews containing more sensory and adjectival descriptors indicate truthfulness.
Reviews exhibiting extremely positive or negative sentiment polarity are more likely deceptive.
Reviews with fewer nuanced emotional expressions tend to be deceptive.
Reviews using more first-person pronouns are more likely truthful.
Presence of temporal markers referring to past experience predicts truthfulness.
Reviews including specific contextual details (e.g., room type, staff names) indicate truthfulness.
Shorter reviews are more likely to be deceptive.
Reviews with repetitive or formulaic sentence structures suggest deception.
Excessive positivity or negativity in language predicts deceptive reviews.
Reviews showing inconsistency in factual details are more likely deceptive.
Reviews with balanced sentiment scores are more likely truthful.
Use of generic or vague language correlates with deception.
Longer, more elaborated reviews tend to be truthful.
Higher frequency of experiential references (e.g., describing activities or events) predicts truthfulness.
